expertQuotes.title: Industry Leaders Recognize Transparency
expertQuotes.subtitle: Expert insights on blockchain transparency and auditability

0.quote: Blockchain transparency is the foundation for building trustless systems. The ability for anyone to verify on-chain enables true decentralization.
0.author: Vitalik Buterin
0.title: Ethereum Co-founder

1.quote: Blockchain explorers are the cornerstone of trust in decentralized systems. The ability to track and verify every transaction eliminates the need for centralized intermediaries.
1.author: Andreas Antonopoulos
1.title: Blockchain Educator & Author

2.quote: Security and transparency complement each other. Open auditability is the proof of a truly secure system.
2.author: Samczsun
2.title: Paradigm Security Researcher

expertQuotes.disclaimer: * Quotes represent general industry perspectives and are not direct endorsements of Quantum Shield.

info.title: About Provers
info.description: Provers are independent verifiers that validate unlock requests. When an unlock is requested, 2 of 5 Provers are randomly selected and must approve with their signatures. This distributed verification mechanism protects your assets from unauthorized unlocks.

notFound.title: Prover Not Found
notFound.description: The specified Prover ID does not exist or has been removed.

fields.totalSignaturesTooltip: Total number of signatures generated by this Prover
fields.stakeTooltip: Required stake as a Prover. Slashed in case of misbehavior

meta.title: Glossary | Explorer | Quantum Shield
meta.description: Definitions of terms and concepts used in the Quantum Shield protocol

glossary.title: Glossary
glossary.subtitle: Terms and concepts used in the Quantum Shield protocol

quantum-shield.name: Quantum Shield
quantum-shield.definition: A next-generation security protocol designed for the quantum computing era. Combines Dilithium signatures with STARK proofs to protect assets.
quantum-shield.example: By locking ETH in Quantum Shield, you can protect your assets from future quantum computer attacks.

dilithium.name: Dilithium Signature
dilithium.definition: A NIST-standardized post-quantum cryptographic signature algorithm. Generates secure digital signatures that cannot be broken by quantum computers.
dilithium.example: When requesting an unlock, use a Dilithium signature to prove you are the legitimate owner.

stark-proof.name: STARK Proof
stark-proof.definition: A type of zero-knowledge proof. Proves the correctness of computation while allowing verification without revealing the original information.
stark-proof.example: Prover computation results are recorded on-chain with STARK proofs and can be verified by anyone.

post-quantum.name: Post-Quantum Cryptography
post-quantum.definition: A general term for cryptographic technologies resistant to attacks by quantum computers. Includes Dilithium, SPHINCS+, and others.
post-quantum.example: Traditional cryptography may be broken by quantum computers, but post-quantum cryptography remains secure.

lock.name: Lock
lock.definition: Depositing assets like ETH into the Quantum Shield smart contract. Locked assets are protected with quantum resistance.
lock.example: When you lock 10 ETH, a Dilithium key is issued, and only that key can unlock it.

unlock.name: Unlock
unlock.definition: Withdrawing locked assets. Requires a Dilithium signature and Prover verification, executed after a waiting period.
unlock.example: After requesting an unlock, you can withdraw your assets after a 24-hour waiting period.

emergency-unlock.name: Emergency Unlock
emergency-unlock.definition: An expedited unlock with stricter conditions. Requires approval from all Provers and a 7-day waiting period.
emergency-unlock.example: If key loss is suspected, you can use emergency unlock to rescue your assets.

waiting-period.name: Waiting Period (Time Lock)
waiting-period.definition: Required waiting time after an unlock request. Usually 24 hours, or 7 days for emergencies. Provides time to detect fraudulent unlocks.
waiting-period.example: If there are no Challenges during the 24-hour waiting period, you can withdraw your assets.

veqs.name: veQS
veqs.definition: A governance token with voting rights. Obtained by locking QS, with longer lock periods granting more voting power.
veqs.example: Locking 1000 QS for 1 year grants 1000 veQS of voting power.

prover.name: Prover
prover.definition: An independent signer that verifies unlock requests. 2 of 5 are randomly selected and generate SPHINCS+ signatures.
prover.example: During unlock, 2 selected Provers sign to approve the request.

observer.name: Observer
observer.definition: A role that monitors for fraudulent unlocks and initiates Challenges. Receives Challenge rewards as incentive.
observer.example: When an Observer detects a fraudulent unlock, they initiate a Challenge to protect the network.

challenger.name: Challenger
challenger.definition: Someone who files an objection against a suspicious unlock request. Correct Challenges receive rewards; incorrect ones incur penalties.
challenger.example: When a Challenger deposits a Bond and successfully challenges, they receive a portion of the confiscated assets as reward.

delegate.name: Delegate
delegate.definition: A representative who has been delegated voting rights by other users. Votes in governance on behalf of delegators.
delegate.example: If you find technical decisions difficult, you can delegate your voting rights to a trusted delegate.

proposal.name: Proposal
proposal.definition: A governance item requesting protocol changes. Can be created by members with sufficient veQS and executed if approved by vote.
proposal.example: A proposal to change the waiting period to 48 hours was submitted and approved by veQS holders.

quorum.name: Quorum
quorum.definition: The minimum voting participation rate required for a proposal to be valid. Usually requires at least 10% of total veQS participation.
quorum.example: If voting participation falls below 10%, the proposal becomes invalid due to quorum not being met.

council.name: Council
council.definition: An elected committee that handles emergencies and makes important decisions. Composed of 9 members elected by community vote.
council.example: If a security vulnerability is discovered, the Council can approve an emergency update.

epoch.name: Epoch
epoch.definition: A unit of time in the protocol. Used for reward calculations and governance cycles. Usually one week.
epoch.example: At the end of each epoch, rewards for Provers are calculated and distributed.

challenge.name: Challenge
challenge.definition: An objection filed against an unlock request suspected of fraud. The Challenger deposits a bond and awaits judgment.
challenge.example: An Observer who detected a suspicious unlock initiated a Challenge, temporarily suspending the unlock.

slashing.name: Slashing
slashing.definition: Confiscation of a portion of staked assets as a penalty for misconduct. Applied to Prover misconduct or incorrect Challenges.
slashing.example: A Prover who made fraudulent signatures had 50% of their stake slashed.

stake.name: Stake
stake.definition: Assets deposited as collateral when taking on a role. Subject to penalty (slashing) in case of misconduct.
stake.example: Becoming a Prover requires staking at least 100 ETH.

bond.name: Bond
bond.definition: Collateral deposited when initiating a Challenge. Returned for correct Challenges; confiscated for incorrect ones.
bond.example: A 1 ETH bond was deposited to initiate the Challenge.

lock.title: 1. Lock

unlock.title: 2. Unlock Request
